narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid background, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection from the law school admissions process—it's a tough moment, and your determination to pursue law is clear. The automated system we use evaluates applicants based on patterns from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, predicting whether someone is likely to pass on their first try. For you, it estimated a 68.4% chance of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that led to your application's denial, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile and signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong academic momentum. Your Year 1 decile of 1st also contributed somewhat to this concern, again standing out as lower than average and reinforcing worries about early performance consistency. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 34 pushed meaningfully against failure, performing better than it might for others and showing your aptitude under pressure. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 offered some counterbalance too, though it was below the dataset average of around 3.21, helping nudge the odds slightly toward passing. Your lower-middle family income bracket added a modest push toward the failure prediction compared to higher-earning peers, while your choice of full-time status was a small positive, aligning with the vast majority who go that route. Overall, your profile ranks lower in key academic deciles than most admitted students, who typically sit in the middle or upper halves, making your situation stand out as higher-risk despite strengths like your LSAT. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant—many in similar demographics succeed with targeted improvements—but the model weighs these metrics heavily to minimize bar failure rates. Moving forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and exploring scholarships or programs that support students from lower-middle income backgrounds. You're capable of turning this around; many have by focusing on these areas and reapplying stronger. We wish you the best in your next steps toward law.
